title: Producing hatching eggs for incubation
subtitle: Selecting, collecting, and storing fertile eggs before setting them in an incubator
excerpt: Hatching success starts before the incubator is switched on. The fertility, freshness, and storage conditions of the eggs you set determine a large part of your final hatch rate. This guide covers what makes a good hatching egg, how to collect and store correctly, and what to reject.
sourceNotes: Gail Damerow, Hatching and Brooding Your Own Chicks (2013). Colin Spence, The Poultry Club of Great Britain, incubation guidance notes. AHDB Poultry, hatching egg quality factsheet (UK).

glossaryTerms.definition: The small white disc visible on the surface of a fresh egg yolk. In an unfertilised egg it is an irregular blotch. In a fertilised egg it is a more defined ring shape, sometimes called a bullseye. Checking for this ring is the only way to confirm fertility without incubating.

The pocket of air at the broad end of an egg, between the shell membranes. It forms as the egg cools after laying. It grows as the egg ages through evaporation. A large air cell means an old egg. For hatching, the air cell should be small and positioned at the broad end.

Shining a bright light through the shell of an egg to check the internal contents without cracking it. Used before and during incubation to check air cell size, detect cracks, and monitor embryo development.

content.text: A hatching egg is not the same as an eating egg. It needs to be fertile, fresh, clean, and structurally sound. The
blastodisc
on the yolk is the point where a fertilised embryo will develop. Everything you do before setting the egg either protects that potential or reduces it.
Making sure the eggs are fertile

You need a cockerel actively mating with your hens. One cockerel can cover up to ten standard-breed hens reliably. More hens than that and some eggs at the edges of the flock may be infertile. Allow one week after the cockerel joins the flock before collecting eggs for setting. Sperm from a single mating can remain viable in the hen for up to three weeks.

To check fertility before you commit eggs to the incubator, crack one egg from the flock onto a white plate and look at the yolk surface. A regular ring shape on the blastodisc indicates fertility. This test is destructive, but doing it on one egg per week gives you a running check on your cockerel's performance.
Selecting eggs for size and shape

Choose eggs that are typical for your breed in size and shape. Avoid very large eggs (often double-yolked and not viable), very small eggs, and any egg with an unusual shape such as pointed, flattened, or narrow at one end. Abnormal shapes indicate shell quality problems and hatch poorly.
Select eggs from the middle of the size range for your breed.
Reject any egg with a wrinkled or pimpled shell. These have thin spots and fail early.
Reject cracked eggs. Even a hairline crack allows bacteria in.
Reject eggs with blood on the shell or any sign of breakage.
Checking shell quality with egg candling
Use
egg candling
to check for cracks the eye misses and to assess the
air cell

. Hold the broad end of the egg against the candler in a dark room. You should see a small, still air cell at the broad end. The yolk should be central. A large air cell means the egg is old. A moving air cell means the internal membrane is damaged.
Cleaning hatching eggs

Do not wash hatching eggs in water. The shell has a natural protective coating called the bloom or cuticle. Washing removes it and lets bacteria in through the pores. Collect eggs at least twice a day to keep them clean. If an egg has light soiling, dry-brush it gently with a stiff nail brush. Heavily dirty eggs should be set aside for eating, not incubation.
Collecting and storing before setting

Collect eggs at least twice a day. Eggs left in the nest in warm weather begin to develop and then die when they cool. In cold weather the developing cells can be damaged by frost. Collect morning and afternoon as a minimum.

Store hatching eggs broad end up in a cool room at 12 to 15 degrees Celsius. This is cooler than a kitchen but warmer than a fridge. A fridge is too cold and damages the embryo. Turn the stored eggs by tilting the tray to 45 degrees each day to prevent the yolk sticking to the shell membrane.

attrs.title: How long can you store hatching eggs?
attrs.body: Hatch rate begins to fall after seven days of storage. Eggs stored for more than fourteen days hatch poorly. The best results come from eggs set within five to seven days of laying. If you need to collect a full batch, plan your collection window so the oldest egg in the batch is no more than ten days old when you set them.

content.text: Warming up before setting

Take the eggs out of storage 12 hours before setting them. Let them warm slowly to room temperature, broad end up. Setting cold eggs in a warm incubator causes condensation on the shell. This is not catastrophic but can encourage bacterial growth. A slow warm-up avoids it.

entries.cue: Fertility checks show poor or inconsistent fertility
entries.cause: Cockerel-to-hen ratio too high, or cockerel is not mating all hens in the flock
entries.fix: Watch the flock for 20 minutes to observe mating behaviour. If the cockerel is favouring certain hens, collect only from those hens for hatching. Reduce flock size or add a second cockerel if the problem continues.

entries.cue: Eggs from the same flock have very different air cell sizes at candling
entries.cause: Eggs were collected at different times and stored for different lengths
entries.fix: Date every egg with a pencil on collection day. Set only eggs within a three-day range of each other so they develop at the same rate in the incubator.

entries.cue: Clean nest box but eggs still arriving dirty
entries.cause: Hens are laying on the floor or in a dirty area before moving to the box; muddy feet carry soil in
entries.fix: Encourage nest box use by placing a fake egg in the box and adding a perch rail at the entrance. Keep the run entrance area dry with a gravel pad or bark chip path.
